notes: CLAMPS1's first field deployment. One of a fleet of mobile PISAs with a focus on low-level jets. Operated on all IOPs and UFOs.
summary: PECAN set out to investigate nocturnal convection initiation, bores, and low-level jets over the Great Plains. It was a large, collaborative project that included NOAA, NASA, and NSF in addition to universities. Operating as a mobile PECAN Integrated Sounding Array (PISA), CLAMPS1 provided continuous thermodynamic and kinematic profiling to study nocturnal boundary layers over the central Great Plains.

photos.caption: Scientists show the CLAMPS1 trailer to a local family during a mission in rural Kansas. An irrigator from the nearby farm drives by and greets the family.

full_name: Verification of the Origins of Rotation in Tornadoes Experiment — United States
notes: VORTEX-USA includes VORTEX-SE and PERiLS deployments. CLAMPS1 and CLAMPS2 deployed in several locations across Alabama, Mississippi, and Arkansas. In PERiLS years, CopterSonde UAS operated alongside CLAMPS.
summary: The VORTEX program supported the construction of CLAMPS2. Designed to profile pre-storm and storm environments across the southeastern and mid-South U.S., this work supported tornado and quasi-linear convective system research under the VORTEX-USA program. CLAMPS facilities supported multiple VORTEX-SE and PERiLS deployments across the southeastern United States from 2016 through 2023. Individual subprojects are listed below.

photos.caption: Data collection domains from various projects under the VORTEX-SE/VORTEX-USA umbrella are plotted on a map of the United States.
All sites where CLAMPS facilities were deployed during various VORTEX-SE/VORTEX-USA missions are shown on a map of the Southeast US region.
Scientists prepare a weather balloon for release next to CLAMPS in Lake Village, AR.
Scientists land a CopterSonde weather sensing UAS near the CLAMPS site in Lake Village, AR.

subprojects.name: VORTEX-SE 2016
subprojects.notes: CLAMPS2 construction was delayed, so CLAMPS1 was deployed in its place.

subprojects.name: VORTEX-SE 2017
subprojects.notes: First VORTEX outing for CLAMPS2; focused on the cold-season severe weather period.

subprojects.name: VORTEX-SE 2018
subprojects.notes: Onboard Doppler lidar failed and was returned to the manufacturer; replacement lidar data provided by NOAA ARL.

subprojects.name: VORTEX-SE/Meso 2018–2019
subprojects.period: CLAMPS1: Nov 2018 – Apr 2019; CLAMPS2: Feb – Apr 2019
subprojects.location: CLAMPS1: Near Courtland, Alabama; CLAMPS2: Near Cullman, Alabama
subprojects.notes: Broader regional coverage focusing on pre-event radiosonde releases across the southeastern US.

subprojects.name: PERiLS 2022
subprojects.location: CLAMPS1: Lake Village, Arkansas; CLAMPS2: Yazoo City, Mississippi
subprojects.notes: Large-scale study of tornado-producing quasi-linear convective systems (QLCSs). CopterSonde UAS flew nearby.

subprojects.name: PERiLS 2023
subprojects.notes: CLAMPS1 was absent due to maintenance requirements. CLAMPS2 co-deployed with CopterSonde UAS.

notes: Nimble project collecting pre- and near-storm observations for numerical modeling. Run concurrently with CLAMPS1 VORTEX-SE; originally CLAMPS2 was intended for the VORTEX mission, but construction delays prevented that deployment.
summary: MiniMPEX was an NSSL-supported project and CLAMPS2's first field deployment, designed to collect targeted pre- and near-storm profiling observations to evaluate mesoscale predictability. This project had CLAMPS operate as a mobile facility, deploying across the Great Plains (mainly in Oklahoma and the Texas panhandle) for hours or less to collect near-storm datasets.

photos.caption: The CLAMPS2 facility deployed for the first time during the miniMPEX project. It is pictured here under stormy skies at sunset; it is so new it doesn't have any logos on it yet.

full_name: Environmental Profiling and Initiation of Convection
notes: Two short deployments: EPIC-1 (calibration/validation) and EPIC-2 (operational UAS evaluation).
summary: EPIC was an NSSL-supported project aimed at providing reference boundary-layer profiles for fixed- and rotary-wing UAS intercomparisons and evaluating the operational value of UAS observations. CLAMPS2 supported two EPIC stints in Oklahoma — a two-day ARM SGP calibration study (EPIC-1) and a five-day Mesonet-site evaluation (EPIC-2).

photos.caption: A NSSL researcher inflates a weather balloon to launch from the CLAMPS facility.

subprojects.name: EPIC-1
subprojects.location: Near DOE ARM SGP central facility (Northern Oklahoma)
subprojects.notes: Calibration and validation study providing reference data for fixed/rotary-wing UAS.

subprojects.name: EPIC-2
subprojects.notes: Evaluated the operational value of UAS observations.

notes: Overseas deployment to Portugal. Deployed in a modular setup in a valley to study flow over complex terrain. Co-located with CU-Boulder lidar and tethered balloon systems.
summary: Perdigão was part of the broader New European Wind Atlas effort, aiming to characterize multi-scale flows in complex (but near-idealized) mountain terrain. This was CLAMPS's first overseas deployment; instruments were shipped and deployed modularly because the full trailer could not be transported. The broader campaign included many sites and a huge number of towers across the valley.

photos.caption: The CLAMPS trailer was too large to ship overseas, so its 3 core instruments were deployed modularly on the ground with a small enclosure for the datasystem.

location: Central Great Plains (Texas to North Dakota; Mobile)
notes: Mobile campaign tracking supercells to capture near-surface measurements of "vorticity rivers."
summary: Aimed at documenting environmental conditions and near-surface vorticity structures in supercells across the Great Plains, CLAMPS2 operated in a mobile capacity from Texas to North Dakota, chasing supercells for the RIVoRS field campaign.

photos.caption: The CLAMPS facility is shown on a deployment in the northern Great Plains; a tornado is visible in the distance.

location: Near the ARM SGP Central Facility (Oklahoma)
notes: CLAMPS collaborated with the U-Wisc. SPARC to collect lidar scans cross-track to the deployment's central RHI along with thermodynamic information.
summary: The project aimed to quantify land–atmosphere feedback and test similarity theory. Boundary-layer profilers like CLAMPS allowed researchers to analyze the surface friction velocity, the horizontal variability of temperature, moisture, and wind, and the variability of surface fluxes over the SGP site. CLAMPS1 deployed near the ARM SGP site for LAFE and coordinated operations with the U-Wisc. SPARC facility.

photos.caption: While deployed near the ARM SGP site for LAFE, a heavy period of rain caused the CLAMPS deployment site to flood; it actually sank in the mud requiring re-leveling of the instrumentation!

full_name: Lower Atmosphere Process Studies at Elevation – a Remotely-piloted Aircraft Team Experiment
notes: Part of the ISARRA annual meeting Flight Week; provided contextual data for UAS intercomparisons via remote sensors and several balloons.
summary: Designed to provide boundary-layer context for UAS intercomparisons and other data collection missions during ISARRA Flight Week, CLAMPS1 deployed in the San Luis Valley to support the LAPSE-RATE experiment as part of the ISARRA annual meeting's Flight Week.

photos.caption: The CLAMPS facility deployed in the San Luis valley for this UAS-centric project.

references.label: de Boer et al. 2020 LAPSE-RATE overview (BAMS)

full_name: Targeted Observation by Radars and UAS of Supercells
notes: CLAMPS2 Doppler lidar was loaned for TORUS 2019 and 2022 (V2 truck, dual lidar mode). Only the dedicated truck lidar has been used on the truck since.
summary: To support supercell targeting with NSSL-deployed Doppler lidar, the CLAMPS2 Doppler lidar was loaned rather than deploying as a full CLAMPS trailer to build out the first prototype NSSL Mobile Lidar Truck for TORUS 2019. That platform informed subsequent dedicated lidar-truck design and operations. The TORUS lidar truck effort collected near-storm profiles of horizontal wind and vertical velocity in storm inflow sectors.

photos.caption: The CLAMPS2 lidar was mounted in the bed of a pickup truck in the first year of TORUS.

After learning about this mode of deployment, NSSL acquired an additional lidar. For TORUS 2022, an upgraded version of the lidar truck was fielded for the first time using the new, dedicated lidar and the CLAMPS2 lidar side by side to evaluate different scanning approaches. In the years since, the lidar truck has operated with just one lidar.

subprojects.name: TORUS 2019
subprojects.platform: CLAMPS2 lidar on NSSL Mobile Lidar Truck (v1)
subprojects.notes: The CLAMPS2 lidar was borrowed for use on the NSSL Mobile Lidar Truck (v1).

subprojects.name: TORUS 2022
subprojects.platform: CLAMPS2 lidar on NSSL Mobile Lidar Truck (v2)
subprojects.notes: Delayed by COVID-19. The CLAMPS2 lidar was borrowed again for the upgraded NSSL Mobile Lidar Truck (v2).

subprojects.name: TORUS LITE / 2023
subprojects.notes: Smaller version focused on airborne UAS data. CLAMPS2 lidar was not used; NSSL truck ran exclusively with its own lidar.

notes: No photos available. No lidar data collected by CLAMPS (loaned to TORUS 2019); not needed at the site since ARM operated its own lidar.
summary: For this mission, CLAMPS2 operated at ARM SGP E41 to provide additional AERI-based thermodynamic observations to expand the existing suite of observations at the site. These profiles were used for the evaluation of micropulse DIAL water-vapor systems.

full_name: Chequamegon Heterogeneous Ecosystem Energy-balance Study Enabled by a High-density Extensive Array of Detectors
notes: CLAMPS1: Near Lakeland airport. CLAMPS2: Near Prentice airport. Deployed in a forested area with several other instruments including NOAA PSL radar wind profilers.
summary: Both CLAMPS facilities deployed in Wisconsin at forested sites alongside many partner instruments as part of the CHEESEHEAD project, which studied land–atmosphere interactions over a heterogeneous forest canopy and investigated how the lower atmosphere responds to spatial heterogeneity in surface energy and water exchanges.

photos.caption: A CLAMPS facility is shown at one of the deployment sites used for this project alongside several partner instruments.

notes: Deployed right outside the National Weather Center between it and the Radar Innovation Lab. This site is very near the operational radiosonde launch site for Norman's NWS WFO.
summary: Conceived after the COVID-19 driven shutdown of all operations to restore operational readiness after extended downtime, this deployment focused on CLAMPS1 assessment, calibration, and post-dormancy readiness testing.

photos.caption: The CLAMPS facility is pictured in the vehicle compound at the National Weather Center.

notes: CLAMPS2: OU Westheimer Airport (full project). CLAMPS1: KAEFS (1st half); Shreveport, Louisiana NWS office (2nd half). Also collected KOUN radar data.
summary: The PBLTops project deployed the CLAMPS facilities to provide boundary-layer profiles in order to validate NWS WSR-88D dual-polarization radar height estimates. Both CLAMPS systems collected profiling data at separate sites to support polarimetric boundary-layer height validation, investigating questions regarding distance to the radar (Westheimer and KAEFS deployments) and environmental conditions (Westheimer and Shreveport deployments).

photos.caption: CLAMPS1 deployed at the Shreveport, LA NWS office during part of this project.
CLAMPS2 deployed at the research area used by NSSL near the Max Westheimer Airport. You can see the original Norman Doppler radar dish in the background along with the KCRI radar.

subprojects.name: PBLTops — CLAMPS1
subprojects.location: KAEFS (1st half); Shreveport, LA NWS office (2nd half)

references.label: Carlin et al. 2024 polarimetric BL height context (JAMC)

notes: Combined field testing, student training, and 4 weeks of scientific boundary-layer data collection.
summary: BLISSFUL was a post-COVID reactivation effort focused on boundary-layer intercomparison, student training, and coordinated profiling at KAEFS, specifically giving students the opportunity to propose and execute their own science missions. Both CLAMPS trailers, the NSSL Doppler lidar truck, a fleet of low-cost surface stations, CopterSonde UAS, air quality UAS, and many radiosondes were deployed at various times to Kessler Farm for combined testing and science.

photos.caption: The CLAMPS facilities both deployed to the Kessler Atmospheric and Ecological Fieldstation (KAEFS), also called Kessler Farm.

full_name: NCAR Lower Troposphere Observing System prototype
notes: Deployed to act as a prototype sensor node for NCAR's upcoming lower troposphere integrated network.
summary: CLAMPS1 deployed at NCAR's Marshall Field site in Colorado to demonstrate the concept of a profiling node for NCAR's LOTOS concept. No field photos from this stint are available. Instead, the photo used here shows the new CLAMPS3 platform deployed at Marshall Field site.

photos.caption: No photos are available from this deployment, however, the new CLAMPS3 facility has recently been deployed at the same site!

notes: The main TRACER mission was postponed due to COVID-19. TRACER-AQ precursor was supported only by CLAMPS1 followed by the main TRACER mission a year later. CopterSonde UAS operated at the CLAMPS2 site during 2022.
summary: TRACER-CUBIC (the CLAMPS team's sub-project) aimed to observe aerosol–convection interactions, sea-breeze regimes, and urban boundary-layer evolution near Houston. To achieve this, CLAMPS1 (inland, Aldine) and CLAMPS2 (coastal, UH Coastal Center) profiled Houston-area sites during the TRACER campaign.

photos.caption: During the main project in 2022, CopterSonde and CU-Boulder UAS operated at the CLAMPS2 site south of the city.

subprojects.name: TRACER-AQ
subprojects.notes: Air-quality-focused precursor campaign to the main TRACER mission.

subprojects.name: TRACER
subprojects.location: CLAMPS1: Aldine (Inland); CLAMPS2: UH Coastal Center
subprojects.notes: Observed sea breeze/urban interactions. CLAMPS2 lidar was delayed and installed later on-site due to its conflict with TORUS 2022.

full_name: Study of Precipitation, the Lower Atmosphere, and Surface for Hydrometeorology / Surface Atmosphere Integrated Field Laboratory
notes: Lidar data is compromised for this project due generally to cold, clean air having too few aerosols to scatter the light.
summary: Aimed at studying cold-season precipitation and surface–atmosphere exchange in complex Colorado terrain, CLAMPS2 wintered at the Brush Creek SPLASH-SAIL site in the East River watershed.

photos.caption: CLAMPS deployed at the Brush Creek site during the winter campaign.

references.label: de Boer et al. 2023 SPLASH overview (BAMS)

notes: CLAMPS2 (E36) considered inflow/upwind; CLAMPS1 (Rolling Meadows Golf Club) positioned between multiple large wind farms. LLNL deployed supplementary ZephIR wind lidars at both sites in year 1. In year 2, the CLAMPS1 lidar's ability to detect wind direction was compromised due to a manufacturer error blinding the orientation system.
summary: Designed to characterize wind-farm wake impacts on boundary-layer evolution over Oklahoma wind farms, both CLAMPS systems deployed to a pair of sites near the ARM SGP region for two AWAKEN seasons, profiling boundary-layer conditions.

photos.caption: Both CLAMPS deployed to the vicinity around the ARM SGP site to study wind turbine effects on the boundary layer.

subprojects.name: AWAKEN 2022
subprojects.location: CLAMPS1: Covington, Oklahoma (Golf course); CLAMPS2: Orlando, Oklahoma (ARM SGP E36)
subprojects.notes: Investigated wind turbine wakes. LLNL deployed supplementary ZephIR wind lidars at both sites.

subprojects.name: AWAKEN 2023
subprojects.notes: CLAMPS1 lidar orientation system failed, making wind direction data unreliable. Manual corrections are required using a coarser nearby reference lidar (A2).

websites.label: U.S. DOE Wind Energy Technologies Office — AWAKEN

notes: Lidar deployed to the roof of the NWC and conducted low-elevation PPI scans in tandem with the NSSL truck lidar (at Westheimer) to develop dual-Doppler signal analysis methods.
summary: Designed to provide a dataset to MIT-Lincoln Labs for developing and evaluating dual-Doppler analysis methods using coordinated scans, this project featured CLAMPS2 (lidar) scanning from the NWC roof while the NSSL lidar truck operated at Max Westheimer Airport.

photos.caption: The CLAMPS lidar was deployed to the roof of the NWC to conduct low angle scans while the lidar truck was positioned at the Max Westheimer airport.

websites.label: NSSL THREDDS — Dual Doppler data archive

notes: Deployed to KAEFS and stayed in place for following campaigns. CopterSonde UAS conducted some flights alongside the Greensight platform at KAEFS.
summary: To supply boundary-layer context for small-UAS weather network demonstrations and flight comparisons, CLAMPS2 profiled at Kessler Farm while Greensight UAS flights occurred nearby. This was the first of a series of three back-to-back deployments to KAEFS (in other words, CLAMPS stayed put). CLAMPS1 operated at the NWC.

photos.caption: CLAMPS deployed to the Kessler Atmospheric and Ecological Fieldstation (KAEFS), also called Kessler Farm, and operated while UAS flights occurred.

websites.label: NSSL THREDDS — PreSCALES Greensight data archive

full_name: Small-UAS Coordination for Atmospheric Low-Level Environmental Sampling
notes: Positioned at KAEFS to provide support to the ISARRA annual meeting Flight Week UAS platform intercomparison and SCALES science missions.
summary: Intended to provide a profiling reference during ISARRA Flight Week UAS intercomparisons, CLAMPS2 served as a reference data source for the SCALES / ISARRA Flight Week campaign at Kessler Farm. It continued to operate through the week of science missions. This was the second in a series of three back-to-back deployments to KAEFS (CLAMPS stayed put). CLAMPS1 operated at the NWC.

photos.caption: CLAMPS deployed to the Kessler Atmospheric and Ecological Fieldstation (KAEFS), also called Kessler Farm, in support of the SCALES project. It served as an important reference datasource for the ISARRA flight week intercomparison.

full_name: Westcoast and Heartland Hyperspectral Microwave Sensor Intensive Experiment
notes: CLAMPS1: National Weather Center. CLAMPS2: KAEFS. CopterSonde UAS also profiled at KAEFS.
summary: To provide ground-truth profiling datasets for simulated satellite instrument overpasses using the NASA ER-2 high-altitude aircraft, CLAMPS2 collected boundary-layer profiles during NASA ER-2 overflights alongside the CopterSonde UAS at KAEFS. This marked the last in a series of three back-to-back deployments to KAEFS, while CLAMPS1 operated concurrently at the NWC.

photos.caption: CLAMPS deployed to the Kessler Atmospheric and Ecological Fieldstation (KAEFS), also called Kessler Farm, to collect boundary layer profiles during the period that NASA's aircraft flew over. CopterSonde UAS also profiled at the site.
